answer:
**Ghalib's famous couplet, from his ghazal "Hazaaron khwahishein aisi ke har khwahish pe dam nikle" (Diwan-E-Ghalib, chapter 70), directly addresses the lover's utter indifference to the boundary between life and death in the throes of love (mohabbat).**[^1]

The original Urdu reads:

> محبت میں نہیں ہے فرق جینے اور مرنے کا  
> اسی کو دیکھ کر جیتے ہیں جس کافر پہ دم نکلے  
> [p-b3e406]

A close transliteration is:

> Mohabbat mein nahin hai farq jeene aur marne ka  
> Usi ko dekh kar jeete hain jis kaafir pe dam nikle  
> [p-b3e406]

The English translation captures it as:

> In love, there is no space 'twixt life and death's dark art;  
> I live by looking at the one who holds my heart—  
> That cruel beloved for whom my soul would die.[^1]

This verse equates living (*jeene*) and dying (*marne*)—there is "no difference" (*nahin hai farq*) between them for the lover. The lover sustains their existence precisely through gazing at the beloved (*usi ko dekh kar jeete hain*), even though this very sight is fatal, as it is the "kaafir" (infidel/cruel one) on whom their dying breath expires (*dam nikle*). Love erases distinction: life is drawn from the same source that kills, rendering the lover indifferent to survival or demise. The heart lives in perpetual tension, thriving on what destroys it, a hallmark of Ghalib's paradoxical mysticism where passion overrides mortal concerns.[^1]

[^1]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, [paragraph](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/70-hazaaron-khwahishein-aisi-ke-har-khwahish-pe-dam-n/translation/#p-d832fa).

args.query: no difference between living and dying
